title: The ATM Paradox: When Automation Creates More Jobs Than It Destroys

source.original: The Lump of Labor Fallacy — Marc Andreessen (X thread)

body_html:
When the automated teller machine was introduced in the late 1960s and deployed at scale through the 1970s and 80s, the prediction was obvious: fewer bank tellers. The ATM could dispense cash, accept deposits, and check balances — the core functions of a teller window — at a fraction of the cost of a human employee. The machines were faster, worked around the clock, never called in sick, and didn't require benefits. If ever there was a case of straightforward technological job displacement, the ATM seemed to be it.

The actual outcome is one of the most instructive data points in labor economics. ATM deployment did not reduce bank teller employment. It increased it. Between 1980 and 2010, as ATMs went from a novelty to a ubiquitous fixture of urban life, the number of bank teller jobs in the United States grew substantially. The economist James Bessen documented this paradox in detail: the number of tellers per bank branch fell, but the number of bank branches increased faster than teller density fell, so total teller employment rose. The ATM reduced the cost of operating a bank branch, which meant it was profitable to open more branches, which meant more tellers were hired — doing different work than before, but more of it.

This is the complementarity effect in its purest form. Labor and capital are not simply substitutes — one replaces the other unit-for-unit — they are often complements, where automating one function makes the remaining human functions more productive, more valuable, and more in demand. The ATM took cash dispensing away from tellers and gave it to machines. But tellers with ATM support became more valuable as relationship bankers, advisors, and problem-solvers for customers the ATM couldn't serve. The freed-up human capacity went upmarket.

The pattern repeats across decades of automation history. The spreadsheet was supposed to eliminate accountants and financial analysts. Instead, by making financial analysis cheaper and faster, it increased the demand for analysis, which increased the number of people doing analysis — doing more complex, higher-level analysis that the spreadsheets enabled. Desktop publishing software was supposed to eliminate typographers and layout artists. Instead, by dramatically lowering the cost of publication, it created an explosion in the volume of published material, which created more work for design professionals, not less.

The mechanism at work in each case has two components. First, automation of the low end of a cognitive or physical task spectrum frees human capacity for the higher end. Second, the cost reduction from automation increases total demand for the product or service category, which pulls more total labor into the industry. Both effects work in the same direction: toward higher employment, not lower, at least in the medium and long run.

The obvious objection is that this only works up to a point. If the ATM automates cash dispensing and AI automates relationship banking, the teller is fully displaced with no upmarket move available. This is a real concern, and it's the more serious version of the AI employment critique. But even here, the historical pattern suggests caution about predicting total displacement. Automation tends to define new frontiers of human-AI collaboration rather than simply eliminating human roles. The surgeon using AI-assisted diagnostics can see more patients and take on more complex cases. The lawyer using AI-drafted briefs can handle more matters and focus on strategy and client relationships. In each case, the automation of lower-level functions raises the ceiling on what the human can accomplish, which increases the demand for the human at the higher level.

The ATM paradox is not a universal law. It is a historically robust pattern that should substantially raise the burden of proof on anyone claiming that the next round of automation will be categorically different. The claim "but this time the AI can do the high-level stuff too" may eventually be true in some domains. But it has been made about every previous round of automation, and it has been wrong every time. The ATM just sits there, dispensing cash, as its paradoxical contribution to full employment quietly continues.
